speaker: interviewer
text: February 2nd, 1996, Rancho Mirage, California. And I'm talking with Paulette Fink. Paulette, we were just talking about your husband getting arrested for distributing this food. Where did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened in a town called Saint-Etienne with a very large population. And you have to realize that we were living completely under ration cards, and what the ration cards was giving us was nothing. For instance, because I had two children, I had a little pat of butter, like you have when you go to a restaurant-- they put a pat of butter on a plate. That's what they had for my-- I had for my two kids for a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that we had nothing, and we certainly had none of the things that he found in this attic. And none of that was ever distributed, because they didn't-- it wasn't mentioned on the ration card. The ration card was a little bit of bread, so much a day, and a little bit meat we almost never saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And any-- all the vegetables were taken by the German, and everything was sent to Germany. We really starved to death for at least two years, where the only thing we had left was what the German didn't want, and that was rutabaga. That's the only thing we had. Don't talk to me about rutabaga today. I couldn't even look at it. We had nothing to eat. I mean, we really were starved. And it had a very strange effect on us, because the women-- the women gained weight. They blew up. They didn't have anymore menstruation, and they blew up. And the men lost weight. The men could not live without meat and without the thing that really they used to eat. And so that the effect on a population was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would do anything to get a meal, at the black market, if you could find one. It was-- it was-- the most intelligent and the most fabulous people-- my father-in-law was a very well-known cancer specialist in Paris and very-- had a fabulous education and with a knowledge, a well of knowledge. I mean, he could talk nothing else but food. That's all we were talking about. So your husband distributed this food and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he-- they arrested him immediately and took him away. And, of course, I didn't know where they put him. And they immediately-- the next morning there was a big poster on my entrance door, on my-- on the entrance of my apartment building saying, I can't translate that. The man Yves Oppert, having disturbed the peace and having act illegally to avoid his own government and the power in charge, will be put away in-- in prison without any-- they date, of course, nothing. He'll just be put away in prison. And it took me a tremendous amount of research to find out where he was, and I finally did, and this I couldn't do anything about. He was there for about four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go see some people to try to get him out? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did-- it happened three times. That's only the first time. The first time it happened, I really didn't know what to do. I didn't know what I could do. Anyway, when he got back, then he abandoned completely any business or anything, and he went completely in the resistance. But not before he took out from that store hundreds of-- of blankets and woolen socks and everything that he needed for the resistance where the kids were going there in the-- went in the summer, and they didn't have any clothes or anything for the wintertime. And so everything he could take out of that store, he did, and he brought it to-- he was like a quartermaster of the resistance at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after about four months, he got out, and he started as quartermaster. How-- How did you join the resistance? How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no such a thing as joining. I mean, that question has been asked to me so many times. There was no such a thing. I mean, you knew somebody who was doing something, and you wanted to help him or her. And I got confronted with the-- with people who were doing the sneaking out of children. And so here and there I was find myself having a few children on my hands and tried to find a place to put them. And so we established a kind of a chain, being tremendously helped by the priest and the nuns who were taking them into monasteries or in their own quarters until they could place them in farm. And this was really-- the women were mainly involved in that. I mean, it was one of the big-- one of the big way you could help. I mean, there were so many children who were abandoned-- abandoned by the Germans, when they took their parents and left the children behind. I mean, and those kids could practically speak no French. And it was-- the biggest tragedy of that time was to have those people who came from Eastern Europe and escaped to France. And France was the haven. I mean, they thought nothing could happen to them when they got to France. They didn't realize that they would be picked up there just as well as they could have been in Germany or in Hungary or in Romania or anywhere else. And they never talked the language. And they were-- they were easy prey for the Germans because they themselves didn't have enough-- enough knowledge of a language. And if they spoke the language a little, they had a terrible accent. So they were immediately denounced. And on top of that, I mean, they lived in a quarter in Paris in a street where a lot of Jews used to live. But I really didn't know very much about it. But when they came from Eastern Europe, they start to go and invade somebody else's apartment. For instance, one family from Hungary would welcome the Hungarian who were coming, and they were always packed up there in that street. And I was told a long time after that that each one of them had a little suitcase with the bare necessity sitting at the head around their bed, so that when the Germans come, they had the suitcase to take with them. I mean, they were such easy prey, and they didn't even try to fight. They were just-- They were just taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children were usually left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Sometimes not, depending.

speaker: interviewer
text: So part of the resistance was to work together and find farms for these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We find place to-- place to put them. And it was good that we knew where they were, and we knew-- we knew the kind of name they had. And they were given the farmer's name, of course. In many-- many instance, 90% of the time, they didn't think that the father was not their parents. They thought they were their children. They didn't know any better, to a point that when we finally were liberated, and when we got those children back, I mean, they simply spat in our face because they heard they were Jewish. They said, I don't want to die. I want to live. I don't be-- I'm not a Jewish person. I am not Jewish. Don't tell me I'm Jewish. And this was one of the greatest problems that we had, because to try to get them by force, get them away, we established those children homes for that reason-- to put them together and be able to educate them as Jewish children and try to go beyond what they wanted, I mean, because they were too young, anyway, to know what it was all about. But they had been fed with the terrible propaganda that the Germans did for five years. I mean, every day there was something against the Jews. There was never a day without it. And so they learned that when you're Jewish, you die. And that's all they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's come back to that. Back in the time, then, of the war, and it's the resistance, these are just young children going to the farms. And how-- what is-- what is your husband doing as part of the resistance? What other things did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What other things? He was-- he was bringing-- he was trying to recruit. And he was bringing boys to the different-- the different place where a resistance had a spot. We were always up in the mountain or in the forest or in-- always hidden. And the only way we could have been found was if the Germans start to go with a-- with a plane, and they discovered a few of our-- our stronghold like this because they did from up there. They could never have found us otherwise. But there was never anything organized. You got into something. If my husband had two or three friends who said, why don't we blow up that factory, or why don't we sabotage this factory, so they would start to work at sabotaging a factory. They would get a few people from the factory to-- to help them to come their way, and they will be doing sabotage. I mean, everything was hit and miss. Everything we did was hit and miss. And we had so many organizations. We had-- the most forceful one was the communist, because the communists were much better organized than anybody else. They were trained for guerrilla warfare, so that they were better organized than we ever were. And we fought practically against them, just like we fought against the Germans. I mean, it was pathetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had so many different groups with different names, until one day de Gaulle, said this is enough. Enough of it. One is pulling here. This one is pulling there. If you have to have one-- one definite-- what shall I say? An army-- let's say an army. It became the secret army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And de Gaulle named them AS-- Armee Secrete. And this was really one-- one overall entity of all the resistance under one leadership-- no more the communists, no more the socialists, no more this, no more that-- one army. All right, so that we were trying to work together. It wasn't that easy, because at Liberation, for instance, the one who was called the FGP, they were the [FRENCH]. They were the communists. I mean, they came out with a red flag. I mean, they went through Paris with a red flag in a car. I mean, we-- we didn't kill that, I mean. But the only thing we did was at least to try to work together during the Occupation. And none of us knew how long it would last. I mean, I don't know if any one of us would have gone on if we had known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- what kind of a hideout were you and your husband in? Where were you most of the time? Were you in the forest? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, moving around-- in Saint-Etienne for quite a long time, and when my in-laws were in the south of France, and the south of France was invaded by the Italians-- I mean, they were under Italian occupation. And it wasn't very bad. But the only thing was that they had nothing to eat. They were absolutely starving because the south of France never produced anything but citrus fruits. And you can't live on citrus fruit. Everything came-- that went always regularly to the south of France-- the wheat and the barley and everything that you need, the flour, what you need to make a meal-- came from the north or from the east. And they were-- they were divided with the line. There was a line in France between the occupied, so-called occupied zone an unoccupied zone. Well, the nonoccupied zone still had Gestapo, but instead of being in uniform, they were in civilian clothes. So they were even more dangerous. But so they called me once in Saint-Etienne after my husband had been arrested for the third time. He called me, and he said to me, the climate there is no good for you. You should really leave. I understood very well what he meant. He said, and the climate here in the south of France is not good for us. It would be nice if we could have a big house and be all together, so please try to find a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 15 minutes after that phone call, they knock at my door, and they say, would you please come and answer questions? So I went. They asked me questions, and I don't know. You always try to think of everything that can be used as an excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, my daughter had a tonsillectomy last year, and the doctor had said that the climate here isn't good. And it's a mining town, so it's true that the climate isn't good. But it will be better for her if she moved away. And, lo and behold, they went to the doctor, and the doctor said, yes, he remembers. I'm sure he never had the faintest notion who we were, but this answered their question. And so we finally left, and after we found a place near Grenoble. We were 16 of us in the same house. And if the Germans had come up there, I mean, there was a good-- that was a good-- in French we'd say a net. The net would have been full. They would have--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were 16 or more of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 Of my family was there-- my in-laws and my nieces, my nephew, my grandmother, [INAUDIBLE], my brother. Everybody was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a big place?

speaker: interviewee
text: A big, enormous place-- a big estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was up in the mountains near Grenoble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was. The most wonderful thing was that we were in a little village where the priest was absolutely marvelous and protected us completely. If it hadn't been for him, we could have been denounced many times. And my father-in-law, being a doctor, I mean, he was constantly called into farms and everywhere to work. And this was one hiding-- wonderful hiding way to-- hidden way to be, you know? We were protected by that already, but if there was anyone who was asking a question about strange people-- I mean, they don't belong here. Who are they-- The priest was always putting his foot down and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name, the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I would. I really wish I would, because he could be about-- about-- about the Gentiles who have helped the Jews, because he was wonderful, absolutely wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the town have a little-- have a name, this little town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izeaux.

speaker: interviewer
text: Izeaux.

speaker: interviewee
text: Izeaux-- a little town near Grenoble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you get around?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bicycle everywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask me again to ride a bicycle. I never could. I mean, we had miles and miles and miles of bicycle-- whenever I went up in the mountain to get some food, whenever we see the-- the peasant didn't have any ration, any coupon-- we had them, but not the peasant, because they were having cultivating their own food, so they weren't getting anything else. But the food that we get on coupon they didn't get. They weren't making that, so if we could give them some of our coupon, they would give me six eggs, maybe. That was fantastic. Suddenly we had a head of six eggs. And so that bicycle was very important. And then when my children need to go to a dentist, I had to go 40 miles to the town, to Grenoble, and put them on my bicycle and go and take them to the dentist. And, I mean, you know, the children really lived with fear all the time, because they were going to school. And the Germans were coming into school. And that was one of their big device.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would very nice and very smiley and then talk to the children. And they would, for instance, say, everybody here goes to Sunday school? Oh, yes. And who here doesn't go to Sunday school? So all they'd need was one kid to sign my children up and say they don't, so that you had to send them to Sunday school. There was no way out.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they found a way. They found a way to penetrate everything and everywhere. And so one day I took my children I was told by the priest to go to school immediately, because the Germans were coming up, and that's the first thing they would do. So go and pick them up quick. So I picked them up. I put them on my bicycle, and we went up the mountain, and we stayed away for three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically people passed messages back and forth and tried to help each other by getting away when there was trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. The people up in the mountain who loved us and thought we were fabulous people, when I looked at a big estate that was underneath, you could see in the valley, and I said who lives there? And he said-- he said, that used to be belonged to Jews. But thank God they are gone. We don't need those people around here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I just simply looked at him, and I said, you know what we are? And he couldn't believe it. For him, Jews must have had horns. I mean, whatever it was, but he never thought that Jews could look like normal people. But this was an everyday-- every day, you risked to be taken. Every day, you risked to be arrested. Every day, they came. They came to pick up a house two houses away from us. Why didn't they come to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there is no way to know why you escape and why you survive. There is no way. All I say is that if the war had last only for another six months, we could no longer go, because we changed our name so many time. We changed our location so many times. We changed everything about ourselves. You could no more hide yourself. It was getting impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many times did you move-- all the time at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I put my children in-- I told you once about the Chambon. I mean, if you ever go to the museum, the Holocaust museum in Washington, you'll see a very good display of what Chambon was.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a little village up above Saint-Etienne, about 15 miles away. The head of that village was a famous pastor, a Protestant pastor by the name of Trocme. He and his wife and the children lived, of course, in the-- in the-- what do you call the house that belongs to the church, where they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were 5,000 villager there. And somebody had heard that someone in that village would hide them. So all of a sudden, one Jewish family came there and went to the pastor. And he gave them to a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that family came to him after and said, what are we going to do with these people? We don't want-- we don't want to be responsible for them. They can arrest us because we're hiding them. We can't do that. So the pastor took a whole bunch of people-- there were 5,000 in that village-- and put them in a big place in the church and talked to them and said to them, now, look. Our duty is to save these people. If they came to us, it's because there is no other place to go to be saved, and you're not going to denounce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm telling you something. We have no weapon. We have nothing to defend them. We can't defend ourselves. All we have are the weapons of our spirit. And it's going to have to act so that each and every one of you feel that it's his duty to do something. And 5,000 villagers hid 5,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Chambon was a fantastic place. And there was a little, tiny children boarding school there. And my children were there many times. Every time, something was wrong, every time I wasn't sure what was going to happen, I would bring them up there. I see. What kind of-- what kind of jobs did you do for the resistance? What are some of the things--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do any job. I didn't do--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry messages?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no such a thing. Sometimes you carried message. Sometimes you were listening to the BBC during the night, and you were getting messages. And you knew who-- who number 2AD was, and you would go the next day to find 2AD and give him the message. That was one of the big things we did. And it was very risky because the Germans were patrolling the streets. And they had special cars that could detect the radio. And so they could-- they knew what kind of channel we were on. So when we were taking message we always had somebody down on the sidewalk going back and forth. If they saw that car they would run up there, stop it, and so we would stop taking the message. But many people have been arrested because they were found taking those message. And so we were always given the instruction that we were told, for instance, that they were going to send a-- a parachute with-- with armaments, and we were told where the parachute would-- would come down. And we'd just go and pick it up. Sometime in our four years of fight against the Germans, they were there before we were. And it also happened that we were getting parts of a machine gun, for instance, in one shipment. And the other part would come in a second shipment. And they would get hold of a second shipment, so you would never could make a real machine gun. That was the life we had. I mean, there was no such a thing as getting orders from anybody. That was much later, of course. At the time of liberation, then there was a captain and a general and a-- but all the time during the war, it was-- there was nothing like this. It was hit and miss, try to do things as much as you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have occasion when you would get messages or hear from de Gaulle himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure. We could-- we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got message-- well, we got message from the BBC. That's the way-- that's the way we were getting orders, and we never could know otherwise. But you had no idea how people got arrested. My husband met a friend of his one day in a cafe in Lyon, which is a very big city in France. And they were sitting in the cafe, and all of a sudden, his friend picked up a cigarette and smoked. And he left the butt-- the butt in the ashtray. And the two of them walked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five minutes later, he was arrested. It was the butt of an American cigarette. How could he possibly get American cigarettes if he wasn't in the underground? So up, and that was it. We never saw him again. That was enough to arrest someone. How did the-- how did it work, with regard to false identifications and names?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to carry false ID. I mean, every time you changed name, you had to carry a false ID. So you always had people-- you had people who were playing double game. For instance, my friend in Saint-Etienne was a man who worked in the police station, but was one of us. And so he had all those identification card, blank. He would take them.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home at night, he would fill them. And he would always fill with somebody alive, which was also very risky. But it had to be somebody way up-- down in the south of France when we used it in the north, so that they never had time to check. But it had to be somebody alive that you could answer, who baptized you? Where were you baptized? All this you had to know by heart when you were taken. And they arrest you easily in the street and asked for identification. So he would get some information on a real person--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --give you an identification and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one department of the resistance. The other one was to get somebody in a police station to sneak out the cards and the stamps and everything and-- and be on our side while working for Vichy. And this is the way I was able to get my husband out the third time he was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that third time I was very, very anxious, because when two Germans had a fight, a brawl, in a bar a few days before, and the first thing they did was to take the prisoner who were in the last basement in the prison. How do you call that when they had no communication with anybody? That was the people they took as hostage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the minute there was something like this happening. And so I was terribly scared to let my husband stay there in that prison. And I went to Vichy, and I couldn't do anything with Petain, because Petain immediately said he was terribly sorry, but if only I wasn't Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave up. And then I returned to Saint-Etienne. And then this friend of mine who was doing the card-- the identification card-- knew somebody there. But he said you're going to have a hard time getting to him, because he's very suspicious. So we had a whole code, and we had a-- a whole-- a whole scenery that we-- that we had mounted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to Vichy, and I asked an appointment with this man. And I had-- I had to say that I came from somebody, which I did. And I was finally introduced to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was very remote. And he wasn't ready to believe anything I said. And I said nothing. But I just said that I heard that a mutual friend was in trouble, and was there a way that we could help?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he still was very suspicious. When I gave him a certain code name and a code number, that started to mellow him. And it was about 11:30, quarter to 12:00, and he said to me, I'll meet you for lunch. I'll tell you where. Here, there, you go, and I'll meet you there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he met me for lunch. And there he opened up. And he said, now, what do you want? I said, this is it. My husband is in a fortress in a very dangerous place, because this is where they take their hostage. And this is-- this is the way our people disappear. And can you do something? So he took mentally the name of the fortress. He looked in his office for the name of the director of that fortress. And in front of me, he called, about 2:30 in the afternoon. He called the director of the fortress. And here is the cabinet of Marshal Petain. We are calling you to release immediately Mr. So-and-so. He must be out by 7:00 tonight. And it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, this is-- this is the way we lived for five years. I mean, things went or didn't go. I mean, the hit was good, or the miss was good. I mean, you never could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we lived constantly in that fear and in that-- in that-- in that kind of a scare-- scare atmosphere. I mean, we heard noise. What was the noise? We were-- we were constantly-- constantly waiting for something to happen. When you think back on it, did it almost feel like you were just afraid for all the time, for all the years? Was there always a certain tension in your body?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were-- depending where you were and who you were. The French people were-- were less-- were less in danger, but not the Jewish-French. I mean, the Jews were always in danger, no matter-- no matter where they-- I lost two cousins of mine with four children who tried to pass the line between the free zone to the occupied zone. They wanted to go back to Paris, and they were picked up by the line and disappeared completely when they-- you just-- the people just went and just disappeared. You never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew it? You knew it was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you knew it could happen to you tomorrow. I mean, there was no reason why it shouldn't happen, but you had to be busy. You had to do a lot of things in order to not-- not to think all the time, otherwise you were lost. I mean, it's a-- the more you did, and more activity you had, then better it was. It was unlivable otherwise. You didn't have enough food. Didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yeah, turn over.
